---
title: Linear Combination
description: Sums of scaled vectors, and how solving a linear system expresses one
  vector in terms of others.
categories:
- Linear Algebra
---

A **linear combination** of $v_1, \dots, v_n \in V$ (a vector space) is any vector of the form $a_1 v_1 + \cdots + a_n v_n$ with $a_1, \dots, a_n \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Examples.**

- $(1, 2, 3) = (1,1,1) + 2(0,1,1) - (0,1,0)$.
- $f(x) = x^2 - 5x + 2$ is a linear combination of $x^2, x, 1$.

**Corollary.** When $n = 0$, the linear combination of zero vectors is $\mathbf{0}$.

A linear system can be rewritten as a single vector equation:

$$
\begin{cases}
a_{11}x_1 + \cdots + a_{1n}x_n = b_1 \\
\vdots \\
a_{m1}x_1 + \cdots + a_{mn}x_n = b_m
\end{cases}
\;\iff\;
x_1 \begin{pmatrix} a_{11} \\ \vdots \\ a_{m1} \end{pmatrix} + \cdots + x_n \begin{pmatrix} a_{1n} \\ \vdots \\ a_{mn} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_m \end{pmatrix}
$$

So solving a linear system is the same as expressing a vector as a linear combination of other vectors in $\mathbb{R}^m$ — set up the augmented matrix and row-reduce to read off the coefficients.
